ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Capsicum frutescens (chilli padi / bird's eye chilli); closest match is hot pepper (whole) FOODON_03301463.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fresh chilli peppers broadly; no specific code for Capsicum frutescens var. chilli padi. Close match accepted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 44027008 refers to Capsicum as a substance; no species-level code for Capsicum frutescens in standard release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chilli padi is 1–3 small pods (approximately 3–10 g), used as a condiment or flavouring in Southeast Asian dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Padi, Raw — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron) taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chilli Padi, Raw (F14040041). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, sugar, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11819 (Peppers, hot chili, red, raw) as HPB does not report these fields for this entry; adaptation documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate from chilli pepper (Capsicum species, raw)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Capsicum frutescens (chilli padi). GI of 23 is an analogous estimate based on raw chilli pepper (Capsicum annuum / Capsicum species) from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (23) × carbohydrate per serving (0.112 g for 5 g serving) / 100 = 0.03, rounded to 2 decimal places. The extremely low carbohydrate content (2.24 g/100 g) means GL is negligible at any realistic serving size.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (9 mg/100 g) and high potassium (377 mg/100 g) with a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Chilli padi is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content and high potassium (377 mg/100 g) support blood pressure regulation, and capsaicin may exert mild vasodilatory effects — however, ensure accompanying dishes and sauces are low in sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low net carbohydrate (2.24 g/100 g), very high dietary fibre (9.9 g/100 g), and capsaicin-mediated improvement in insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chilli padi has a low glycemic index (~23) and negligible glycemic load per serving, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; capsaicin has evidence-based properties supporting improved insulin sensitivity and reduced postprandial glucose, though benefit is modest at typical condiment serving sizes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.18 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (9.9 g/100 g), high potassium, and capsaicin's cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chilli padi is appropriate for individuals with cardiovascular disease; its very low saturated fat, high fibre, and high potassium content support lipid and blood pressure management, and capsaicin has been associated with reduced LDL oxidation and improved endothelial function in peer-reviewed studies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade classification (Capsicum frutescens); solanine-related alkaloids may trigger joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chilli padi is classified as a nightshade vegetable and is rated unsuitable for RA-sensitive individuals; nightshade sensitivity is individual — track personal response carefully and substitute with ginger or turmeric if sensitivity is confirmed.

llm_training.natural_language_description:
Chilli padi (Capsicum frutescens), commonly known as bird's eye chilli, is a small, intensely pungent chilli pepper that is a cornerstone condiment and flavouring ingredient across Singapore, Malaysia, Thailand, Indonesia, and the broader Southeast Asian culinary tradition. The raw pods are firm and slender, typically 2–4 cm in length, tapering to a sharp point, and transition in colour from bright green when unripe to vivid red upon full maturity. Both colour stages are used in cooking, with red pods generally considered hotter and more aromatic. Chilli padi registers approximately 50,000–100,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU), placing it significantly above jalapeño peppers and making it one of the hottest chillies commonly used in everyday Southeast Asian cooking.

In Singaporean hawker culture, raw chilli padi is served whole or sliced in light soy sauce or fish sauce as a universal table condiment alongside dishes such as Hainanese chicken rice, wonton noodles, and congee. It forms the spice backbone of sambal belacan, chilli crab sauce, laksa paste, and numerous curry bases. In Malaysian and Indonesian cuisine it appears in rendang, nasi lemak sambal, and ikan bakar marinades. Thai cuisine uses it extensively in nam prik dipping sauces and green papaya salad (som tam). The pods are consumed raw, sliced, pounded, blended, or briefly stir-fried, and are rarely the primary ingredient of a dish — instead functioning as an intense flavour amplifier used in quantities of 1–10 g per serving.

Nutritionally, raw chilli padi is low in calories (57 kcal per 100 g) and exceptionally low in net carbohydrates (2.24 g/100 g), while delivering a remarkable 9.9 g of dietary fibre per 100 g — one of the highest fibre densities among common vegetables. It is an outstanding source of vitamin C (143.7 mg/100 g, approximately 160% of the adult daily reference intake), which supports immune function and collagen synthesis. Potassium content is high at 377 mg/100 g, contributing to electrolyte balance and blood pressure regulation, while sodium is negligible at 9 mg/100 g, yielding a highly favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio. Saturated fat is very low at 0.18 g/100 g, and cholesterol is absent as this is a plant-based food.

The primary bioactive compound, capsaicin, has been extensively studied in peer-reviewed literature for its roles in improving insulin sensitivity, attenuating postprandial blood glucose elevation, supporting lipid metabolism by reducing LDL oxidation, and exerting mild vasodilatory and thermogenic effects. These properties make chilli padi clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease, provided it is consumed as part of a balanced diet and accompanying dishes are controlled for sodium and saturated fat.

Chilli padi belongs to the Solanaceae (nightshade) family. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to solanine-related alkaloids should monitor their personal response carefully, as nightshade vegetables may aggravate joint inflammation in susceptible individuals. The food is inherently vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: Is chilli padi good for diabetes?
How many calories in chilli padi?
Is bird's eye chilli low carb?
Can I eat chilli padi if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of chilli padi?
How hot is chilli padi in Scoville?
What vitamins are in chilli padi?
Is chilli padi good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14040041), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Secondary micronutrients (saturated fat, sugar, vitamins, fatty acid profile) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11819 (Peppers, hot chili, red, raw), a closely matched approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for raw chilli pepper (Capsicum species). Ontology codes are defensible closest_parent matches with gap notes provided.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14040041 (Chilli Padi, Raw) — exact match. 2. Saturated fat (0.18 g), monounsaturated fat (0.09 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.86 g), trans fat (0.0 g), sugar (1.5 g), cholesterol (0.0 g), vitamin C (143.7 mg), vitamin A (48 mcg RAE), vitamin B1 (0.09 mg), vitamin B2 (0.09 mg), vitamin B3 (1.24 mg), vitamin B6 (0.51 mg), vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg), folate (23 mcg DFE), vitamin D (0.0 mcg), vitamin E (0.69 mg), phosphorus (43 mg), magnesium (25 mg), zinc (0.26 mg), selenium (0.5 mcg), and water (81.0 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11819 (Peppers, hot chili, red, raw) as HPB does not report these fields for this entry. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central NDB 11819; plant foods do not naturally contain vitamin B12. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central NDB 11819; raw plant foods do not naturally contain vitamin D3. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; naturally zero in raw plant foods. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. 7. GI of 23 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for raw chilli pepper (Capsicum species); no direct GI measurement for Capsicum frutescens (chilli padi) was found in the approved GI database as of 2026-04-16. 8. Dietary tag 'wholegrain' from input cultural.dietary_tags removed as it is not applicable to chilli padi. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301463 (hot pepper, whole) is a closest_parent match; gap note provided. FoodEx2 A00LC (chilli pepper, fresh) is a closest_parent match; gap note provided. SNOMED CT 44027008 (Capsicum substance) is a closest_parent match; gap note provided. 10. No -1 sentinel values remain in per_100g; all fields populated from approved sources.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'close' to 'closest_parent' to align with VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md requirements for g5_ontology_honesty.

Iteration 3: Substantially expanded natural_language_description from ~1,247 characters to ~3,200 characters to address q6_llm_ready_semantics failure. Added detailed coverage of: physical appearance and Scoville rating, specific dish applications across multiple Southeast Asian cuisines (hawker culture, Malaysian, Indonesian, Thai), capsaicin bioactivity mechanisms, comparative nutritional context, and comprehensive dietary flag summary. Added additional common_user_queries and meal_context entries. Added ingredient synonyms (siling labuyo, prik kee noo).
